You are editing JoRap garden notes at /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com

TASK: Apply Feynman technique to each note in data/feynman-batch-4.json (43 notes).

For EACH note listed in that JSON file:
1. Open content/english/notes/{slug}.md
2. In `key_concept:` block, INSERT a new 2nd bullet that explains bullet 1 in plain language a grade 6 student (age 11-12) could understand.
3. Keep bullet 1 unchanged. Push existing bullet 2+ down (they become bullet 3+).
4. If bible shortcode {{< bible >}} exists in key_concept, keep it and any blank line; first `-` bullet is still bullet 1.

Grade-6 bullet rules:
- One complete sentence (can use two short ones with a dash)
- Plain words only - no wikilinks [[...]], no jargon without a simple swap
- Concrete image or analogy when it helps
- Not condescending - just simpler
- No contractions in frontmatter
- End with period
- Do NOT duplicate bullet 1 - simplify it

Use StrReplace on each file. Work through ALL 43 notes in the batch.

When done, return: count of files edited, any skipped notes and why.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
All 43 notes have been reviewed.

Drafting grade-6 simplifications for all 43 notes. The first note is "practice-small-experiments": "Big-bang rollouts look decisive and usually snap back by March."

The second note uses a plain analogy: Jesus did not pick His closest helpers from a list of pros and cons. He prayed all night on the mountain before naming them. The grade-6 bullet must avoid wikilinks.

Drafting the remaining grade-6 bullets. The third note simplifies John 17 as the longest prayer in the Gospels, where Jesus speaks to God about Himself and His followers. The fourth note uses the feeding of five thousand as a concrete example: after dismissing the crowds, Jesus went up the mountain alone to pray, treating exhaustion as a cue rather than an excuse.

The fifth note contrasts Jesus' public teaching about prayer with His private prayers to the Father. The sixth note advises preparing, documenting, and testing before an emergency strikes.

The seventh note frames following Jesus as daily surrender of one's will, not theatrical suffering. The eighth note compares pressure to a stress test, revealing weak spots that calm days keep hidden.

The ninth note protects the most important task from inbox rewrites by blocking email first. The tenth note says one hero sprint cannot survive a bad week; a steady routine is better. The eleventh note suggests joining the group's framing before redirecting blame.

The eleventh note adds that listening to the group's perspective before redirecting blame prevents jumping straight to fault. The twelfth note uses a highway map analogy: without a clear destination, every exit looks equally
</details></think>
